description:
You are acting as the “artifact‐manager” for a small binary-repository farm that is stored under

    /home/user/artifacts/

Your objective is to introduce *one* user-level systemd timer that deletes “*.tmp” files older than seven (7) days from that directory every night at **03 : 17**.  
The timer must also write a single-line audit entry to a log file so that automated tests can verify the job actually ran.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA – The automated grader will check the following exact items.  
Make sure *all* paths, file names, unit names and log-line formats match **exactly** as stated here.

1. Script file  
   Path:  /home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh  
   a) First line (she-bang) is exactly:

      #!/usr/bin/env bash

   b) Somewhere in the script there is an **uncommented** line that starts with:

      find /home/user/artifacts -type f -name "*.tmp" -mtime +7 -delete

   c) The very last line in the script must be **exactly** (no additional arguments, no leading/trailing spaces):

      echo "$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S') - cleanup executed" >> /home/user/artifact-cleanup/validation.log

   d) The script must be executable by its owner.

2. Service unit  
   Path:  /home/user/.config/systemd/user/artifact-cleanup.service  
   Minimal required content (case– and spacing–sensitive):

      [Unit]
      Description=Artifact repo cleanup

      [Service]
      Type=oneshot
      ExecStart=/home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh

      [Install]
      WantedBy=default.target

3. Timer unit  
   Path:  /home/user/.config/systemd/user/artifact-cleanup.timer  
   Minimal required content (case– and spacing–sensitive):

      [Unit]
      Description=Daily cleanup of artifact repo

      [Timer]
      OnCalendar=*-*-* 03:17:00
      Persistent=true
      Unit=artifact-cleanup.service

      [Install]
      WantedBy=timers.target

4. Enablement (symbolic link)  
   After enabling the timer, the following symlink must exist:

      /home/user/.config/systemd/user/timers.target.wants/artifact-cleanup.timer  →  ../artifact-cleanup.timer

   (A relative “../” link produced by `systemctl --user enable ...` is acceptable and will be what the grader expects.)

5. Log directory & first run proof  
   a) Directory /home/user/artifact-cleanup/ must exist.  
   b) File /home/user/artifact-cleanup/validation.log must contain **at least one** line that matches this exact regular expression:

      ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - cleanup executed$

   A quick manual run of the service (`systemctl --user start artifact-cleanup.service` once the units are in place) will satisfy this requirement.

6. No crontab entries must be used for this job. The grader will reject the solution if any line containing the string “cleanup_artifacts.sh” appears in the user crontab.

Hints (do NOT output actual commands here):
• Create the directory structure first.  
• Place the script, set its permissions, then author the service and timer unit files.  
• Reload the user systemd daemon, enable and start the timer, and fire the service once manually to populate the log.  
• Confirm the symlink path in 4. exists.

When you are done, the entire solution should only have required three to four shell commands beyond the text-editing steps (creating directories, writing files, enabling the timer, and launching a first run).

The grader will inspect only the artefacts listed above, nothing else, so please follow the specified paths and content verbatim.
truth:
Directory tree and files that must exist **after** the agent finishes:

/home/user/bin/
└── cleanup_artifacts.sh   (mode 755, owner user)

/home/user/artifact-cleanup/
└── validation.log         (contains ≥1 audit line)

/home/user/.config/systemd/user/
├── artifact-cleanup.service
├── artifact-cleanup.timer
└── timers.target.wants/
    └── artifact-cleanup.timer → ../artifact-cleanup.timer   (symlink)

Exact file contents the grader will check:

1. /home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh
--------------------------------------
Line-0: #!/usr/bin/env bash
(may have comments)
One line MUST start with: find /home/user/artifacts -type f -name "*.tmp" -mtime +7 -delete
Final line (literally, last line in file) MUST be:
echo "$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S') - cleanup executed" >> /home/user/artifact-cleanup/validation.log

2. /home/user/.config/systemd/user/artifact-cleanup.service
-----------------------------------------------------------
[Unit]
Description=Artifact repo cleanup

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target

3. /home/user/.config/systemd/user/artifact-cleanup.timer
---------------------------------------------------------
[Unit]
Description=Daily cleanup of artifact repo

[Timer]
OnCalendar=*-*-* 03:17:00
Persistent=true
Unit=artifact-cleanup.service

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target

4. /home/user/artifact-cleanup/validation.log
---------------------------------------------
At least one non-empty line; every line must match:
^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - cleanup executed$

No crontab lines containing “cleanup_artifacts.sh”.

Permissions:
• /home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh — 755
• All other files — 644 (default umask OK)

The grader will resolve the symlink to ensure it targets “…/artifact-cleanup.timer”. If the link is absolute it will still pass, but a relative “../artifact-cleanup.timer” will be preferred.

Systemd does not actually have to *run* inside the container during grading; file presence and contents are what is evaluated.
